speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From her American Ridge childhood, Willa Cummings Carlson has strong loving memories of one neighboring pioneer family above all, that of Fanny and Al Roberts. Among the least liked people in early Troy were certain members of the Hayes family and she recalls some of the events that swirled around the shooting of Marshall Hayes and around his daughter who became a madam in Lewiston. She also discusses J .P. Vollmer, Idaho's first millionaire, and her own experiences with learning as a child.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: First, I was wondering about what you had heard and what you knew about the importance of the railroad, coming to the railroad for Troy. Oh, well, that was terribly important. When my father came,

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: and his name was Charles Cummings, when he came out from Michigan, he came by rail as far as Colfax and the railroad had stopped there. Now I have in my notes someplace, or should have had, if they're not taken out and put some other place, and I didn't think that had happened, how excited the people of Moscow were waiting for this to come. But I think that you'll find there in one place where Mrs. Roberts had said in her diary that Mr. Roberts' people, family, were coming from Pennsylvania. Well, I think they stopped by way of Illinois and Kansas. That's the way most of that particular group of people came. They stopped at these places because there were people who had gone ahead of them to those places and they stopped and visited for a while and then came on. And they were so excited about them coming from Colfax, and I remember it especially, I took the notes down from Mrs. Roberts' diary. Oh, I just could shake alpha for giving that up. It was all right for Ann to come and visit. I'm not saying anything about her getting it, borrowing it or something of that sort, but to give it away so now she has nothing. I said, you are the biggest fool I ever heard of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, did she give it away completely?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, that's what they said. Oh, I didn't know that. And you know, because Ann has made use of it once and she really shouldn't make use of it again except, well, she's got different viewpoints, but she's a factual gal and I don't mind seeing a little extra flesh draped on the skeleton. I never do. I think it makes it more interesting. And Mrs. Roberts had plenty of places where you could do that because, and of course I've heard her tell those things so often that I know even what her, what the things were that she would put in. You know, I knew that. Well, her writing was horrible and very hard to make out. So at one time, Ms. Helm took it. It was a very good penman. And she took it and rewrote it and rewrote it with ink that lasted well. So that part of that diary is, that has been rewritten was very clear and very plain. And it was in this part that telling about the excitement they had about getting Mr. and Mrs. Roberts, that is, Al Roberts' father and mother out here and then his brothers who came with them. And that was Cyrus, Frank, Frank I guess was the oldest. And that was the first time that Frank, Cyrus, and Oliver, Oliver was the young brother. And those three came and they came over to come to Colfax. And he went to Colfax. A snow came. It was in the spring. But a snow came but it turned out to be, of course they weren't used to the country and the climate, and it turned out to be a real summer snowstorm. And so before he got to, oh I think along about Palouse, he had to trade the bob sleds for a wagon and went on the rest of the way with a wagon. And then he had to bring them home in a wagon. And I don't know, he didn't get the sleds home until the next winter I believe it was the first time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the Chinook came up, right?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, you don't, I expect a Chinook. I believe she said that it was a Chinook. They always say it's a Chinook when it goes off. But you get snowstorm in the last of April, usually in the way it used to be. And certainly in May it was like when Watts disappeared. They go off and they don't need a Chinook to take it off. Just naturally the sun's up a lot higher you know. And just ordinary temperatures have been rising. And unless they rapidly fall they're not going to keep on the snow. No snow lasts when they come that way. That is up to date. Of course there may come a time when they will, I don't know. Can't say about that. But anyway, they came home in the wagon and I always thought that was quite interesting how they went up there sledding them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what about the railroad coming to Troy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well the railroad that came then, that you see, they hadn't come to Troy yet then. So it came later after the old Roberts' had come out. And I don't know, I believe I have it in my notes what year it was. And my father came in 1889 and it was after it came that it went from Troy down to Kendrick because he worked on the railroad line between Troy and Kendrick. And I'm not exactly sure one day it got into Troy. But people in Moscow were terribly excited about the rails coming, the train coming. And they began to get excited down the line. Troy was a tough little town and got tougher I guess. It was a tough pioneer town and then it got tougher as it got to be a railroad town because it was a railroad headquarters for quite some time. The headquarters for supplies for the railroad company as it built the line on down toward Kendrick's. Down through that canyon. That was really something after driving it down there. But by that time they were around with cameras that took pretty good pictures. Not complex. I had cameras and Dan Helm who married Ressie afterward. Well he had a camera and went around and took pictures. We used to have one of them but one of the other kids got it and the pictures were divided up and I felt lucky getting a smashing machine picture. But anyway, they took pictures and showed the boxcar where they ate down the mountain canyon. Hard to find a wide enough spot in the bottom of that canyon to set their boxcars. Mrs. Roberts went down there and cooked one summer for the men. And she told how many she cooked for. Well I don't remember that but I'm sure that was in that diary. No, I don't know that it was in the diary. I think it was in some little additional booklet. You mentioned it in your manuscript that she did cook for the men. Yes, I know. But of course I knew a lot of that by her telling me because I just ate up this stuff. I just lived along with Roberts when I'd be allowed to. I'd have to be brought home. I'd be down there listening to all this stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that like for her to cook? Did she say?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh yes, she was a pioneer woman as I said in my book. When she got started she said I'm going to be a pioneer woman. And she lived it to the full. Mrs. Roberts was a woman who loved life. And she lived it. She was a wonderful person. And Mr. Roberts was the kind of fellow that he lived life to but he loved life I mean. And he lived it up to but he didn't have her persistence and he didn't have that... Well, he wasn't as stable in his likes and dislikes as she was. And he had a more mercurial temperament you know. He was up in the clouds or down in the dumps. And she wasn't. She understood that you had to take the sunshine with the shadow. And of course she never did put that across to Mr. Roberts quite. Never had a clue. All the way across. They were a very delightful pair of people. And very entertaining. And in that day and age when you depended on what people said to entertain you, they were in great demand. Always. And Al Roberts was always in great demand. He was a county assessor. He liked to be a deputy assessor because he got out on his horse. He always had a riding horse. And had these big saddle bags carrying the paraphernalia that went with the deputy assessor's office. And he'd carry them along. And he was always very welcome in all the homes. Come on in and stay Al. And then when Al got ready to leave, well if ever you come by my place you know you just find out ever you're on American Ridge. Find out where I live. Everybody knows I live in the middle of the ridge. And you're perfectly welcome to stay all night. And they had lots of lodgers. Took advantage of that. Many of them. They never knew when they were going to be alone. They had lots of people coming in. And that was the kind. And when he died I couldn't go to the funeral. We lived over in Idaho County at the time. And I remember my husband being away on a cattle drive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get cold?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No, but it just makes a lot of noise and I can't hear you. Oh, I see.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Isn't my voice clear today?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, it's pretty clear but it's still it's... There's a lot of crack. Sometimes I think that my voice gets cloudy. Because people say to me on the telephone, you don't sound clear. And I can project my voice. You sound clear. Know what I'm doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it about them? Can you expand on what made them entertaining to other people?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Personalities. And the way they lived. You find a person that loves life and sees life in all its angles. And more than just a common ordinary clodhopper. Excuse the term. When they tell things, it's much more entertaining. And besides, you know, there's all the difference in the world between one person and another telling a tale. There's how many people, lots of people just see things they say the way they are. That's an awful way to be. Then there are people who look at things through rose -colored lenses. Well, they're not always the nicest things to have around either because they're always too happy maybe. And people get a little bit disgusted with them for being so terribly pessimistic perhaps. Well, you know, the difference between a pessimist and optimist is droll. The optimist the donut sees. The pessimist the whole.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: There's that difference. Some get the meat of it and see the whole thing. As I've said to my, as I had to say to a man who came to school to see me about why his boy didn't get his good grades in an English class is his daughter did. He said that was a good article and it was, yes, I said, and I know it was. It was a very good article, but he tried to take the extraordinary and make the ordinary out of it and that wasn't what he was supposed to do. He was supposed to close that extraordinary in words that would satisfy his audience and create the atmosphere, create an atmosphere of certain things. I said that was what we were trying to do and we were trying to see which one got the best. So the ones that made it the clearest, there's no way for any teacher in a grade school and especially the way they used to grade to grade them evenly and fairly. I recognized that factor and always said so, but the way we graded them, the way we were required to get grades, if we were going to have to grade them, aside from saying this is very satisfactory or giving them a few words, they didn't want words, they wanted letters, they wanted a real grade. But I said now she isn't that way. If Tommy wants to grade them, he said well that year my brother started the school and he walked along with me and the girls went along with the girls. They went to the same school, but they went along with the girls. But I said Marilyn didn't say it that way. She said the day my little sister started the school, I took her by the hand and it was a beautiful day. I'll never forget the day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The sun was shining so brightly and I talked to her about it and she said well isn't the sun shining because I'm going to school?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And she said I told her yes, it was shining for her. She said we went down and she said I felt so proud and when we passed people on the way she said I'd wave and she'd wave too. We waved so gaily at them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she'd say to me do they know I'm going to school and this is my first day of school?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I said all the kids sat up and took notice and they could see little Susan doing that. There's a difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said no, do you live by the fact alone or do you also take an impulse on the prairie?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well you don't, you're living a lot of life. Well now it's that time. I was just waving this stuff to them. And Mrs. Roberts really had a sense of the... Oh yes and she also had a sense of the time that it was. Yes. She knew that. Oh yes, she was great. And she lived out there in that little old house. It was quite a log house.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: They had a house with a lot of the first frame structures. It was never painted. It was unpainted but we objected to her ever painting it. But I couldn't go to the funeral.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: My husband had been on a trip with cattle to Spokane and he came home and he said well do you want to go?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I could take you down tonight to the train. I said you could go over in the morning. You'd be late but then they'd be glad to see you. And I said no I can't go. Something was wrong with the children. One of the children I don't remember. I knew I couldn't go and I said no but I'll just write to them. So I wrote to Mrs. Roberts and of course that was a very personal, very private letter but I did say to her that for Mr. Roberts I always will remember Samuel Foss's poem, Let Me Live in My House by the Side of the Road. You'll remember that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I wrote a stanza of the poem and she wrote back and said who did you say wrote it and how will I get it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: She said I want to have it copied on Al's tombstone and it's out there on his tombstone. And that's what he thought. He didn't care for anything else. And he always went hunting every year. He went hunting and he always took one fishing trip and he had such glorious time at it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He came back and he said now what will Frank May get out of life?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I knew but I don't believe I said it then that he had a pretty large size ten bucks full of mortgages, a mortgage on almost every firm on American Ridge. They took them out to show them to me. I don't know why somebody was always parading as a cheapest to me. I was a young girl at the time. But anyway, they worked there. The only girl they had to death.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's this now?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Frank May. Dora May's father. Poor old Dora and didn't get anything out of life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Al said what's he going to get?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Frank May is going to get out of life. He never had a day of fun in his life. He's never had one day of fun. And he said now look at me. I don't have a lot of money. But he said my crop turned out pretty good. Got a dandy stand of alfalfa up here on the hill. The hogs will eat well next spring. He said and we've got chickens and if nothing else he said Fanny will keep those chickens going and we'll butter an egg money. He was always full of fun.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Fanny, that was Mrs. Roberts. Her name was Frances Elizabeth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did Frank make, come by the mortgages?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He bought them. He had the money. He had a little money when he came there. He never spent a cent on anybody. He never gave away a cent. And I understood that he had gambled and got some stuff in the early days buying gambling. And that might have been fraudulent. I shouldn't be surprised. I wouldn't say it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And for heaven's sake you don't have that thing on now do you?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Me saying that you can't. Well it does matter. I wouldn't want to say that about him. But I don't know how he got it. But anyway. And then they would come to him and ask him for money when they were in a tight spot. And he was very good about letting his neighbors have it. But of course he didn't let them have it free of charge. They paid the usual amount of interest, whatever it was. And nobody held it against him. I don't hold it against him if he had the money and he did them a favor of loaning it to them. But I said then to Mr. Roberts, well you get fun out of different things. He gets fun out of getting another mortgage and putting it in the box and nearly filling it up with his money. Now that gives him deep inner satisfaction. And you get deep inner satisfaction when you get away from the grizzly without the grizzly getting you up in the hill. That gives you a deep moral satisfaction. It would give me deep satisfaction too. But I don't know if it would have anything to do with my well being except that I was alive yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Al ever tell you any of the kinds of things that happened to him out on the expeditions in the backcountry?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh he was always telling about something running into a grizzly at night or something. He wasn't as good a storyteller as Mrs. Roberts. If she'd been up there she could have made him more dramatic. But he had some dramatic tales and people used to come in to listen to him telling him. I didn't think he dressed him up near enough. You know, really. They were pretty matter of fact. They were telling about how they went to bed one night, tired out or wet and soaking wet out in the storm and they'd been going all over one place or another. And they heard an awful noise and the horses were squealing and they knew something had happened and wondered what in the world was the matter. One fellow said that's Bear. He said if they smell Bear they'll make a noise just like that. That's not rain or no rain. And got their rifles loaded and they hated it. They cleaned their rifles at night as tired as they were. And they went out and there were three big old grizzlies. And they didn't know. They didn't usually go in threes. They never saw that pair. You could see once in a while. And they didn't know they'd gotten into grizzly country and that's what one of the guys said. We got into grizzly country. Well it so happened that they decided the grizzlies had gotten off their beat and had come down there. But Mr. Roberts assured us that they got all three of them. I don't know if they did or not. And they, he had all kinds of trophies you know. Like moose horns and elk horns and all kinds of little deer horns. But of course he sold a lot of those to people that came from one of them. But their houses filled him. Mr. Roberts got good and tired of having them around sometimes. The moose horns stood up on the organ. They had an organ and they had to stand there. She'd have to clean them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you don't need to clean them?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: The moose did clean them all the time. He said that's alright let it go. And I don't suppose he would have bothered about it but she would. How did Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Roberts tell stories that made her story so special?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Because you don't often hear of real good woman storytellers. Oh, you do too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a male, what is a shy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: A shy Mr. You. I'm not. I'm not. I like to hear it when I do. Let's put it this way. Nowadays, nowadays I think, at least I haven't run into too many yet. You're one of the best. Well then I'm sorry for you because you haven't heard anybody very good then. I'm not especially good at telling stories. I don't make a habit of telling many stories. Of course, children in school. I've been a school teacher. And they love stories. And they've surprised me sometimes by, oh please tell this story again. Please tell it. And they're tickled to death. Well I'll tell you one thing that they liked about me was I do have a sense of humor. And most kids like my sense of humor. Now and then there's one of them next to me. But then I, as I say before, I believe in teaching children. And when I see that there's a youngster that objects to my storytelling, may I tell it, I try another attack. I attack him a different way and I try another approach. And pretty soon I find what he likes. And after that I had him. I did a certain amount of experimenting. Well what about Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Roberts? How did she tell her?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well she was just her own self. And she just told the way it was. She told the way it was and then she had her ringing laugh. And she'd laugh and tell how this thing came out, you know. And, oh I know I used to ride with them. They'd take me along. And he was one of the first ones on American Ridge to get a car. And I don't know sometimes it was an old second hand car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind was it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Maxwell. A Maxwell car. One of the very first kinds of cars that was ever put out. And it was high. Gee whiz you had to step up on a step and then you had to step to get up into the thing. It had no top on it or anything. And the steering wheel, you know, was imposed upon the car and here it was out in front of him. And he'd sit up there, you know, I've seen pictures of him of the old times. You know those cars that looked like that and they did look so funny. And the man would sit up there and look so high. He was always offering me a ride. My folks didn't like to have me ride with him. Papa said that he was going to scare a teen to death one of these times and scare him into running away and there was going to be a mix up. And Al would be killed and so would I if I was alone. But I still survived.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But I'll tell you sometimes he did. I had some trouble in that car. Well usually Mrs. Roberts went along. She didn't mind. She liked to ride. She was daring. And I loved to go. I'd go anytime I got a chance and sometimes I didn't have the chance. They would let me go and if I could get out of asking, well I'd get to go. So I remember that we'd ride in that car and go places and then Mrs. Roberts along. Sometimes she'd see something in the road. And I'll be careful. We're coming to this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when we got there and it didn't amount to anything maybe, you know, well Fanny what do you think of that now?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And then he'd ask her, you know, she's out of it. I think that was a black man standing there in the road. And just the way he'd say it to her and her answer. And she'd kind of laugh. Of course she was sort of embarrassed and yet at the same time amused too. She couldn't help being amused by him. And I don't know what made her story different except that she herself was different. It's the individual that talks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you explain if you compare her to say what a lot of other people were like?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: How she was not specific, some special people in particular, but I mean just what it was that set her off. I still try to understand. She dared to live. She dared to say what she thought. Men were talking politics and saying a lot of things that she knew as foolishness and if they would have admitted it they'd know it. But she would chime in with some little remark that actually she was not a woman. And actually, if they'd have only known it, cut them off at the base. And it would be rather funny to anybody that was looking on and listening and knew exactly what had happened. And Mr. Roberts would feel piqued. Now, oh they just had lots of little jokes in between them and would always tickle people. Now my folks lived right across the road from them and they used to come. In those days people went visiting a great deal. You never knew on Sunday morning when you got up you'd better be prepared. You'd better have a cake baked and a little baking done. If you didn't you were sure going to be surprised because you didn't. And certainly you weren't to be caught without light bread. Now you couldn't run down to the store and buy a loaf of bread. Had no way to run down to the store. It took the team a long time to get there, especially ours which was a work, a team of work horses. Mr. Roberts had a pretty good driving team. Cayuses that I think he'd bought from the Indians. But anyhow, used to have old Buck and Rowdy and they were buckskin horses. You know what that is. It was a funny brown tan tannish brown horse. Oh Buck and Rowdy. Rowdy lived much longer than Buck and Buck and I had a colic and I was supposed to keep out of the way but I remember watching through the cracks of the board fence. I watched many things through the cracks of that board fence. And my brother who was four and a half years younger than I of course didn't want me ever to go without him. And if I would be taking care of him and they always trusted me to take care of him I sometimes got by with things that I never would have gotten by with. He hadn't been with me. But he was a nuisance. And I would help him up a couple of runs on the boards on the fence. Had lots of board fences. And they also had a rail fence. We used to come up on that rail fence watch things. We were perfectly safe when they're looking out through. But one time we went down to look when they were getting a hunting party ready and we weren't supposed to look. And there we were looking through the boards of the fence so interested that we forgot to try to hide ourselves. And so we showed and enough of my face showed us who I was. And of course as soon as they had that picture ready and showed it around everybody just laughed. My hair was well on the oracle. We were having a good time. We saw the hunting party off. But my mother didn't know what to do. She saw the fence. Saw it in the picture and so such things as that happened. And a lot of them were from running off to that down. Of course it was just a little way down to Mr. Roberts. Of course he'd come Papa down there yelling for us every now and then. We'd have to show up. But we knew enough to stay out of the way. They said we'd get hurt down around among those pack horses. You see people came from all around. There were Chicago men and especially two people. Two men, one by name Ford Robbins and one named Dr. Whittaker who was an M .D. from Chicago. They came out to Moscow. They knew somebody by the name of Monroe Hibbs up here at Moscow. Now I only knew him through seeing him at Mr. Roberts' sometimes. But they would come out and of course they had plenty of money to buy a good camping outfit. And they would come getting their camping outfit and Mr. Roberts was to wrestle horses and he'd get these Indian ponies or some kind of ponies and they'd have 11 or 12 ponies. Did you say Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Roberts wrestled horses?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, not in the sense that he stole them. I mean he got out and hustled and rounded them up. They rented them or bought them. They had money to buy them. He didn't know. I don't know. All my illusions about the wonderful Roberts were suddenly going to be excluded. Oh, no, no, no. He didn't steal anything. Nothing. Absolutely not. They were in some nice country. Well, it would just be a crime. It was a crime then. Believe me it was a crime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So these two guys would come out and he would pack them?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He'd pack them but always with two or three people from Moscow. Monroe Hibbs would always be along with them because they were friends in Monroe. And then one summer I know Mrs. Hibbs and Mrs. Bob West who was another man from Moscow that meant nothing came and stayed with Mrs. Roberts while they were gone. And they thought those women would love it out there in the country. Get away from town for the summer and get away from the city.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I called it a city. I remember that. I thought it would be grand to come up here and stay in the city a while. I wouldn't want to be out in the country. I think I thought there was a lot more going on in the city than there was in the country. I guess that's what those women thought. But anyway, not so much Mrs. Hibbs but Mrs. West.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And Mrs. West, I heard her saying one day, well, I'm supposed to, Bob is supposed to give me and he comes back if I stick it out, stay out here the full two months he's gone and to get a diamond ring. And I won't settle for less than a $250 diamond because this diamond I got didn't cost that much. I remember saying that. My goodness. I'd have to be looking for somebody that could offer a ring that cost more than $250 if I was going to be in the swim of things according to Mrs. Robert West. All those things like that are always coming up, looking back at me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can I ask you how you grew to be so close to the Roberts? I know that they were neighbors but is it that you just hung around the place and got to be like part of the family?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, they only had two daughters and those daughters were married and had gone away. And I had little problems. As I say, my childhood was not free from problems and I never discussed them. They're not part of the story. But they did have a bearing on my story as far as that's concerned. Mr. and Mrs. Roberts just sort of adopted me as a third girl and they often said that. She's our third daughter. Very often. And they took me places and they did things for me. And my folks were contented with a bread and butter existence if I may be excused for saying it about them. They worked hard. My father worked his fingers to the bone. And I learned, thank God, to accept him for what he was and honor him for what he was because he grew a garden that was the horticulturist's dream. Actually, it was just like a park. But how I did resent the fact that I had to work in it all the time digging out the weeds and keeping it like a park, a beautiful park, and he cultivated it and all that. I wouldn't milk a cow. That was one of the rural things that I missed because I was afraid of cows. And I stayed afraid of cows even after I got big enough that I could have overcome it if I had really tried, I'm sure. I've overcome worse things. But I did have to work outside and I didn't like it. I didn't relish it. And Mrs. Roberts knew that and she always tried to make me feel better about things. And Papa had built up things and he had an eye for beauty that I never realized until I was grown. But he did have an eye for beauty. And setting it up on the outside, he made lots of pictures, you might say, out of the things we had, the trees, the orchard, and it was kept so beautifully. And it wasn't so bad. But he was uneducated. He was an intelligent man, but he was uneducated. And he was unable to see how my terrible longing for reading mattered. And if I were peeking through the bars of the fence to see something more interesting going on than what I was living, I was snuggled up on the corner reading a book and I was maybe with a couple of English orphans crossing the high seas and in the home where they suffered and I used to cry when they were in trouble. Or I was some other place. Well, Mrs. Roberts knew that and she gave me, she would get books for me. But then so did everybody else in American Age. I'm a product of American Age. I had lots of people. I was known as a book lover and so they loaned me books, people who had them. Not many people had a library, you know, in those days. And it was a mighty nice thing that they did. And I remember that one family had, they took McClure's magazine. Of course that gave out a long, long time ago. But my it was informative. So I was practically on speaking terms with the Astors and the Gools and the Fanderbilts in their early days and how they built up and also learned a little bit about the economics of the country. How they got hold of the stock in the railroads and built up the railroads. Well, I learned a lot of things when I was a youngster reading and using. And as I said, Mr. Muschles, I had him five months in the year, but believe me, what he taught me and then I had another teacher, Carlton French, who was a very good one. Now, Carlton French, I expect you run into his name too. He's Burton French's brother. Burton French's brother, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And is that thing on now?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I don't want to say anything about him now. I'm not going to say anything about him. He didn't amount to very much, however, as a representative, no matter what they say. Idaho was rather proud of him because he was from North Idaho and I'm sure they were kind of proud of having him. But Carlton French, I think, was a far better man than Burton who went as a representative to Washington, D .C. Carlton lived and he always remained poor. He was always kind of a country school teacher. I don't think he ever went beyond that, but he had had academic work at the university and I don't know how far he'd gone, but he was self -educated and he was a great reader and he was a nice fellow. He married a girl up there around Troy and they had a lot of children, but of course they didn't have that when he came. Yes, he was married. Had one little girl when he taught our school and he taught around the neighborhood and taught others and he was a good school teacher. And I believe that was the first year that we had seven months of school. They decided on American Age to give an extra month and then different people sent in their children from two or three school districts around, only one or two ready to take the examinations. He gave the eighth grade examination twice and I remember that it was the first one. I took the examination and I wasn't quite twelve years old. But I'd been taking everything at school. I listened to all the classes and I was excited with them and I didn't know a thing. And he was like, Mr. Muscious, he'd let me. Mr. Muscious would let me. Not all teachers would let me. I could keep my mouth shut when the other class was recycled. But anyway, I took that examination and at that time, we'd never had reading taught as reading. I mean, going into the meaning of it. So, along came the examination and it was about a lot of things, about the interpretation of reading. I hadn't been taught the interpretation of reading. And if I'd have known what it was, I suppose I would have made a better stab at what I did. Anyhow, Mr. French said I would probably make a passing grade. I'd probably, I might make seventy. He had to pass, then it'd be passed. There were county examinations in those days. We didn't have it like it is nowadays. But we had to make seventy. Well, I tell you, I was worried about that. And that's the meaning of so many big words. And I'd always been a terror for words. Sometimes I liked to have words, but some of these had me floored. We hadn't had the readers. You know, the teacher didn't have time to teach eight grades and teach everything and then have a reading book and teach you out of the reading book. So we got through the fifth grade reader we never read anymore. What reading we got would have to be along with the English. Once in a while, a poem or something like that. At that time, I didn't read my poetry very well. I didn't know what poetry fiend I might be in days to come. So he looked over the paper and he said, well, he made out that I would get about sixty -eight or nine and they might possibly give me seventy. If he were grading the paper and knew me, he would give me seventy and I'd pass the examination. But I had to make an average of eighty -five and I'd have an awful hard time making the average. Oh, I didn't want to get an eighth grade diploma on a low average. No siree. He didn't want such a thing. So I told him I didn't want it. So he decided he wouldn't send any papers in. And there was only one other paper that would do it all and he told her he knew she wouldn't pass. And that was Faye Mushlitz, a daughter of Mr. Mushlitz, who was teaching in another school but nobody to take the examination down there. So he sent her and a couple of boys from his school over to have it in our school. I said that they were all sending, the neighboring schools sent their candidates for the examination over to take it. Well, I went home that night and Papa was so angry. I never saw him so angry and he never was so angry at me because I had said I wouldn't take that examination. It was the first time I knew he cared whether I passed it or not. I was horrified. I said, well, if I hadn't known one day so bad I guess I would have done it. But I can do better next time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how do you know?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: You'll probably know a lot worse. Oh, he said a lot of terrible things.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But as I say, it was the first time I knew he cared. He cared, as I learned. Well, the next examination, I think that was in either the last of February or the first of March and the next one was in April. All had to be early for rural schools, out early. And the next one we had more people come. That's when Carl came over and took the examination with him across the canyon. The second examination I guess. That's my uncle Carl. He's dead too. One of my guys' brothers. And that was the first Carl's neighbor to come across the canyon. And he kidded me the day of his death every time he came up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How's the inhabitant thereof?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He always used to say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Inhabitant thereof?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, nobody could understand it if you didn't know what it was. We were supposed to. The examination was all based, the whole thing except one question that we had to tell something about reading. Why we lacked it or something like that. I don't remember what that was. That faded into insignificance beside this other one that had about twenty -eight or nine questions. It was all based on the chambered nautilus. I'd never heard of the chambered nautilus or thought I hadn't. And then I said, oh, I have heard of the chambered nautilus. I know it was written by Oliver Wendell Holmes and he was the autocrat of the breakfast table and it's written in the autocrat of the breakfast table. And Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: French said to me, had you actually read that?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes. I said, it's in the autocrat of the breakfast table.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, are you sure?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, how do you happen to be sure about the autocrat of the breakfast table?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Because I said, I got it as a prize last year for winning head marks in spelling. And I didn't like the book a bit. Autocrat of the breakfast table, I thought it was the silliest old thing I'd ever read. But because it was my book and I'd gotten it as a prize, I read it. And strewn through the book, a lot of Oliver Wendell Holmes poems. And it included the chambered nautilus. And I didn't know what a nautilus was, but there was a little note that told about it being a fish. And it was seafood and the nautilus, I changed it, you know, building more stately mansions on my soul. It was as if seasons roll. We'll each build a new mansion with a dome more vast than that which now we, oh I can't repeat it now exactly like I did. I kind of lost my touch. Well anyway, I knew what it was. Well, he looked that up, but a whole class there, there were twelve people taking the examination and twelve people couldn't look at the fine print of Oliver Wendell Holmes' book and get it. So he said, well we have to go to town. We have to go to Troy. They got the, down there, and we'll get the book and send the eighth grade book, read, use it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And we'll get, huh?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: What

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: lead?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Off of the pot and just take a knife and stick it down at the side and it comes up to lift the area of the lid. Just stick a knife down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do it? Did it come?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: That's all. It's very simple. Just knowing how is all it takes. In fact, that's what takes most things. Just knowing how.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, so you went down to Troy and got the phone? Well, how were they going to get to Troy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: We were six miles out, seven miles, close to seven miles. Our place at that time, the way the road went, was about seven, a little over seven miles out. So you were halfway between Troy and Henry. And it was about fifteen miles from Troy to Kennedy. And so the school, we weren't very far from the school, but it was about six, three quarters of a mile to town. And Carl Carlson, that was my husband's brother, of course they didn't live on our age, and they'd had to cross the canyon, and they'd come across horseback. And so Carl and David, David Swanson maybe. Now, I don't really remember, just sort of lately. Well, you know, Dave worked for us for the, for those three years. I know more about it than I know about it. You know, well, but I'm sure you know quite a bit about Dave, because we just thought of him as one of the family. Carl and Dave came across the canyon horseback. And, uh, he asked which one could go the fastest, and Carl said, oh, mine can. And Dave laughed and said, oh, I suppose his can. I guess so. Dave, you know, he'd let Carl have his way. He was one of that kind. And, uh, so, uh, French had his brother there, his brother, Frank French, had happened in to visit that day. He had a school someplace, but he didn't, wasn't getting, didn't have to give any examination, so he had to tie him off. And he came in to visit his brother. And there was some other teacher there that had come with his pupils. So, Carlton left the school with those two, and we took the geography examination or some other examination. Well, he went to town, wrote Carl's book and got those books. And he got six or seven books. They couldn't let all our readers go. I think they didn't have readers enough for the whole class, but they were taking me for the examination in town, too. I had to take them in small towns, too. They had to take these state examinations in those days. So, he went to town, got them, he came back. That pony went fast, too, I tell you. Carl said, you're riding past,

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: you know, the books. And we had to get together and read. I know I had to read this here for others. And then, Frank says, well, now all the people in town have had, they've had a lesson on this. They've had the book, and they had a lesson. They've read it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Did he ask them?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And yes, they'd had it. But that they wouldn't be willing to let us take it and read it carefully. But he said, we've not opened the questions, and he held up the questions, and the men that were there, both men teachers, other men teachers, including Miss Frank, Frank's brother, said no, they'd take their oath on it. He hadn't had it opened. So, all right. He said then he'd give us, I don't know, 10 minutes or something like that, study that. Well, I got hold of a dictionary, and I was sure mad who he was giving me to take this. So, Carl said, I'd like to take it over here. He had a notion that I'd be fast with it. He kept it all his life. That was the answer. So, he and Dave were sitting together. They had to sit in one of these double seats like we used to have in the rural school. And I sat with, oh, I don't know who it was, somebody, some girl.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Can't remember who it was. And then, there was somebody behind us, I think we had five or six together on a book. So, we'd read a question, and they'd be getting up their answer. You had to think up your answer and write it out. Branch told us it would be a good thing if we wrote it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I'd write it, what did you write?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I wasn't very much interested in what anybody else wrote. If I just got a chance to write at it myself, it was not all I cared for. But anyway, it's, you know,

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: what is it now in the chambered nautilus where it says, see, building more stately mansions on my soul. That's apostrophe, dressing your soul with it as a person.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And at some place, the tenant says something about the tenant, the tenant was lately removed. Nautilus has gone out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about the inhabitant thereof?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: That's what we're hearing about. Well, I'll come to it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: So, French says, he's asking us questions, he says, what is the meaning of tenant?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I'd already looked that up, believe me, I didn't know what tenant was. I know what I learned. I didn't know what it was. So, I looked it up, I had a dictionary, and I was looking up, oh, I was looking up fast. And I was remembering too, believe me. I knew I didn't dare forget them, I knew they'd ask them those words. And I says, the inhabitant thereof.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: We didn't have any big dictionary, they were just small and dictionaries, didn't amount to very much and didn't have a very good definition.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I had an idea, what do you think the tenant was?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, in here it was a fish, I knew it was a fish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how does it apply to you? Could you be a tenant?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Sure, if I lived in something, I'd be a tenant. I lived there. I inhabited a place, I'd be a tenant. I knew that, I was pretty mad at him asking all those questions. And Carl was snorting, you know, he was laughing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I said, did you know what it was?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No, he didn't know, you'll answer the next question. I don't care, I know what it's going to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it, he said, what's it going to be?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I don't remember what the next question was. I said, Carl, you go ahead and answer it, I won't. Well, he didn't know the next one neither, and so he had to turn it down, his chance. And he, I remember that he said many times that he cuzzled his brain to remember what the next question was because he didn't know it either. I don't know, I wasn't worried about the words. I got them. The ones that I mean were in the questions that followed the poem. But that's what the inhabitants there are.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you pass that time?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes, with 96.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what some of the reading matter was on the Ridge? What kinds of books people had and the ones that you read?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, we had a library during the school, didn't amount to very much, but we had one, had water babies, Alice in Wonderland, Alice with a baby, and we had a boy that got taken in by drugs and took him into the saloon and got him to drinking. That was Willie, and I think he got killed, and before the story was over, his mother felt so terrible about it. It was kind of a sad story. The old Lamplighter took the little girl in, and that was the main part of the story. And I remember that her name was Gertrude, and I don't remember much else about it, but she would have been without anybody at all. Her mother, something had happened to her mother, and that left her lonely and defenseless and without anybody. And so he took her in, and he had a friend, that is a woman, who came in and did some work for him. She also kept a house for somebody else, the only way she could keep her body and soul together, the way she lived, and he was the Lamplighter and an old man. None of them, neither one of them made much money, and she had Willie. She had this boy, and she had to take care of him because he was lazy and as I remembered. And I don't remember much about that, but this little Gertrude, that's the way she got taken care of, and the Lamplighter always took care of her.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And she found a job, and she took care of him at the end. It was all very sad. I remember I used to cry and cry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they have Shakespeare at all?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes, I think they did. Have a small volume with something about Shakespeare in it. But I really didn't become very well acquainted with Shakespeare then, in that Earl School, not until a little later. You said that people had their own private libraries too, not many books, but different families had a few. Oh yes, I had free reign. I could go any place I wanted in the neighborhood and borrow books. I was told I could come. And if my folks would allow me, there were places where they wouldn't let me go. I guess it was false pride. I didn't have it, believe me, I had to remember that there were certain places where folks didn't let me go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there families that they didn't like very much?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I guess so. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard to get around to different places on the ridge from homestead to homestead?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I had a pair of the best walking legs in the country. And I remember that some man, and I can't remember who it was, said, well you're a regular little Mercury, if you were my little girl I'd call you Merc.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, why would you call me Merc? He said, have you ever heard about Mercury?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Mercury do?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I remember saying, he walked here and there, but I said he had feet. He had wings on his feet. Well he said, you do too, you walk all over. And I often thought about that, that I was Mercury when I was little. And I was sent... I don't know, it reminds me, just that you can tell Sam another time about walking out here. Yeah. Or living there. Yes, that was a character that, I don't know where she came from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she on the ridge? In Troy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: When she was out of Troy. She came out of Moscow. I don't know where she belonged really. But she'd been out here to the Lundkus, you know, around Moscow. And she seemed to be an offshoot from the Lutheran Church, I think. The Swedes. It could be. It was always the Swedes where she went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that where she was called both?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes. Go to that, because she walked all over the country. She walked all over and she leaped on Cottons when Cottons would go by. Well I didn't know that, I don't think that. I think that's just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what made her character? Well that would make her a character, don't you think?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: You don't see very many people walking along the road and refusing to ride. I think you're mistaken there, maybe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she rode with Daddy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh yes, she rode with Daddy after they had assured her. And called up Mrs. Kelberg. Well she wouldn't get inside cars. She'd be brought in a hood or a running board or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what was she like? As a person?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I don't know that anyone was very close to her, but I think that she was related to... I shouldn't say that, but I think that she was an ancestor of someone like Wilfred Olson. Oh no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Don't you think so?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No. He's dead now. Mrs. Wilfred Olson lives there alone. That's Warfred. Wilfred. Yeah, that's what I mean. Wilfred Olson. Well I don't know about Wilfred. I don't know anything about his ancestors. But Wilfred is not dead, surely. Well I don't know about Wilfred. I'm talking about Wilfred.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is Clearwater number six, C6?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yeah. These people that were wearing this trailer are from C6.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that about?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I've been going to ask you if it was Clearwater. Ronnie and I figured out that C Counties the other night and figured it must be Clearwater. Well anyway, that's an interruption and I can't tell you anything about Lea. I don't know anything about it. I just know that she was afraid to ride with strangers and they would tell about it. She spent her time on the highway. But she did. In odd clothes. Whenever she could walk, when it was walking weather, she walked. And I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, who keeps her?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And well, she always had a little money. And I think that the Lutherans kept her. I don't know. It seems to me I knew that, but I'm not sure. So I don't know that they did. And she used to come out to Kelberg's and she would stay there long enough to, oh, have a good bath and clean up. Stay there a little while. She'd stay with them. And she would want to take off the pillowcases and the sheets and sleep on the mattress and on the bare pillow. And Mrs. Kelberg said, she explained to her, well we put the sheets and the pillowcases on so we can wash them. But, you know, we can't wash the pillow and the mattress every time somebody sleeps on them. She had a lot of time getting that into her head. I get the idea she was slow. She was a slow learner, I suppose. Maybe a retarded one. I don't know. I didn't come in contact with her. I've seen her on the road, but I didn't know her. So I know that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'm not going to tell anything about anybody. Of course, you can get what you please now. But I don't, I mean, I'm the local government there, you know, because he was a town marshal. Heaven only knows why or how, but he was so mean, the old man. And he drank and then his triplet sons, they drank and everything else. And I was too young to know personally, know them. But later on, I got acquainted with the old lady, Hayes. That was after the marshal had been killed many years. And she had, as I told you then, as I told you the other day, she had those three children. She was bringing out Eleanor and the two boys, Johnny. And I don't know the name of the other one. I was trying to think of it the other night after you've gone. But I don't know the name of the other boy. But they were nice, mannered boys and she was bringing them up. And she said, well, her daughter. And she named her. I don't know what that girl's name was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what, Mavis, I said they were on that, I believe they did name them on that article about the old safe, didn't it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'm not sure, but I believe it did name those girls. But anyway, I'd forgotten about what Mrs. Hayes' daughters were named. I told you about the one who married. Well, she had married first this man, DeFrancis, had the DeFrancis Hotel. And then he died and she continued to run the hotel. And I don't know whether they had run it as a house of ill fame or ill repute, as they called it, before he died. Or whether she just instituted that afterward as the widow. But she run it there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, was the hotel in Troy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No, that was in Lewiston. That was in Lewiston. But that's where she met.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does he mean?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I was forgetting it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Lanquist?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Lanquist, L -A -N -D. Lanquist. He and his father lived in Lewiston. They were from Sweden and he was a Swedish aristocrat. Belonged to the aristocratic class. Well, he was a high -end mason, a high -degree mason. 33rd isn't it, I guess that's what it was. And he was the, whatever the head man in the mason logist is. That's what he was at the time all this happened. So it nearly killed him. And that's when this son went to see his girlfriend, the woman that he was about to marry. And she was a school teacher. And a very good school teacher that made a wonderful reputation. Or had a wonderful reputation in Lewiston. And they had, I think, set the wedding date. It was to be that June, in June. And then he made a detour on his way home where he was with the boys. And they got him to go with them down to this hotel. Which was a disaster. But I never did feel sorry for him. I don't feel sorry for anybody who gets caught in a mess like that. Unless they're given a drug and taken in bodily. As some people have been. It was done sometimes. Somebody would take them in and then when they woke up there they'd be in this awful hole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what happened?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, they found him in bed the next morning. Or he found himself in bed with this old woman. And he'd been married in the night. She'd called in, of course she had her contacts around town. She could do anything she wished. She had money and she'd made a fortune down there. She'd made a fortune. And she had these two greyhounds to back her up. Nebuchadnezzar, I don't know what the other one was. Some other big Bible character. I remember those two outlandish looking dogs. Great big funny. They were hounds. I said greyhounds maybe. But they were some kind of hound dogs. Big ones, you know what. Those fancy ones. Never had them anywhere but on a leash. But she went with those dogs for protection. Instead of revolving her pocket. I don't know about that but I suppose so. Anyway. She had decided she was going to live a life of respectability. And she wanted this up and coming young dentist. So that's who she decided on when she got him there. And he fell into the trap. I don't think she went out to get him. Or maybe she had sent these fellas out to get him. I don't think so though. I think he went in. As far as I know of his own free will, he went in with the boys out having a time.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And she lived to regret it of course. Well, she got in an officer in, you know, to write up a license. Even though it was a Sunday night. I heard it was a Sunday night. I guess it was. But whether it was or not, it was all... He made it legal. She bribed people to make it legal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say that he was to get married the next day?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No, not the next day but in a few weeks. Lord, here this awful story came out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was Landquist?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Landquist. And Zella Morris. Never married. And I'm sure it wasn't for love of that though. I'm sure she wasn't the kind that ever wasted a moment on any man like that afterward. But then she just decided she'd have another kind of a life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what happened to Landquist after that?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, I told you. He went from bad to worse. She made him stay in Lewiston a while. And I guess he drank himself to death and his father was about to shoot him, I guess. Oh, now I'm supposing. The father was the one that he'd just nearly killed. I don't remember what he eventually did but I think he left town. Probably went back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me that he was eventually Dennis and Troy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Landquist was?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes. Of all places to come to. He came back to her. Well, I wouldn't say her birthplace. I don't know whether she was born Troy or not. I don't think so. The Hayeses had come to Troy. I don't know how old she was when they came to Troy. But they had these two girls and then the boys. And I think the girls were older than the boys. She was an old woman by this time. Great, big, robust looking thing.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And they said that the Hayeses were part Indian. They might have been. I don't know. She really looked like an Indian princess. A lot of people called her Pocahontas. She went with beads, long beads, and lots of big, flashy, gaudy jewelry. This woman. And she had a sister. Well, Mrs. Hayes anyway said that one of the girls had offered to take those children of Henry's. But she said she wouldn't allow them to raise those children. She was going to raise them right. And she did. Eleanor was a nice girl. She'd asked me if I could help her some and I did.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I was just in high school then. Troy. And the boys were nice kids. And Paul went to California. Oh, that's right, before we were married. He went to San Francisco. And he got a job out at Half Moon Bay. Construction job of some sort. And he got hurt and had to be... Well, he lost his job or gave up his job. He was in hospital for a few days and then came back home. And I was in high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But anyway, while he was there he wrote that, who do you suppose I saw on the boat as I came down?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He went on the boat from Seattle to San Francisco. He said Johnny Hayes is a steward on that boat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that one of the original older kids' grandchildren?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes, one of those grandchildren that Mrs. Hayes was going to raise right. And I've heard, I did hear one time, I used to try to keep track of them because I wanted to know what happened to them. Eleanor married a fellow. I don't know. She went to school someplace. I believe the grandmother died. And I'd been away from there. That was after I was married. And she left the house to these kids. And Eleanor came back. Her husband was going to manage the sale of the house. And they had a raffle in Troy. They questioned whether he could do that. And he raffled off the house. You buy a ticket and see if you got the house. And Paul got several. And I thought it was foolish to buy that house. I said you don't want it. I wouldn't have that house. Anyhow, I don't want that thing. Oh, well you might get this for nothing. Daddy was a great guy. To want things for nothing you paid a dollar for the raffle. And I think he sold about $2 ,500 worth of tickets. And the house they said was probably at its best worth about $1 ,200.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He sold a lot of tickets. But he couldn't sell them. I believe he sold an all day sucker with him. To catch the sucker. That's what he caught. But a fellow named Seth something or other. I remember his first name was Seth. Seth was a teacher in the schools. 7th and 8th grade teacher. And he got the house. It was genuine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me about when Lunk was the dentist in Troy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: You said that he was afraid of her. Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mentioned that last time?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes. She had told him. He said he would leave. He was going to get out. She was afraid he would try. And she told him she'd kill him if he went. And that he didn't have enough money to take him around all over the world. And that she didn't care where he went. Across the world or around the world or any place where he went. She thought I would kill him. And he figured she meant it. And I suppose she did. But anyway, eventually after being at a number of places. I don't know where all. I heard he'd been up at Stites and up the river until he put himself out. And then people heard he'd been diseased and they didn't want to have anything to do with him. Especially the dentist. I wouldn't have gone to him for anything in the world. But they said he was a very fine dentist because his father wasn't satisfied with anything the best. So he'd been trained in the best schools. And he was a very good dentist. When he came to Troy and got a friend of the same old. The room was there. The dentist stood occupied. And he got a girl named Virgie Skeems. She's a friend of mine. I knew her a little enough to be very friendly with her. As an office girl. She was a very nice girl. And she wasn't a real young girl. She was older. She was older than I. Quite a little bit. I suppose five or six years older than I was. And by that time I was married.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: So Virgie was the office girl and she said. Virgie said that every day when the train came up from Nilliston. At that time we didn't have buses. But we did have two trains a day. And it was a great sport to go down to the train. Meet the train. Meet the train. Young and old. But especially young. Or the idle old. They were the ones that went to the train. Of course the people who were doing the real work didn't go. And so this happened. But he was in the drug store. And the drug store had a door that went right on through. And the local telephone office was upstairs of that building. The drug store was in the downstairs. And the doctor's office was across the hall from the dentist. And the dentist was there. Some other rooms up there for other offices. But I worked there. I worked as a... I was a new evaporator.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: So Virgie said he'd go out and stand. There was a back stair that went down. Down the back stair. And on top there was a kind of a... I don't know what you call it. I call it a porch. That extended out. And a railing around it. A very crude thing. But nevertheless that's what it was. We used to go down that back stair lots of times. And we were going someplace and took a shortcut somewhere. And for anybody to know that we'd gone, we'd go out that back way instead of going out the front way and down Main Street. And that thing went clear through. That was one of the biggest buildings. It was a frame building. It was the biggest building from Troy at the time. And he'd go out there and stand on that. Or he'd stand on the door and look. And watch until everybody that had gotten off the train came up off the platform and uptown. And somebody had said something once about watching who came or something like that. And he said... Well there was one thing about he never said... And she noticed that. He never wanted a date. An appointment with anybody at that particular hour. He left the dog. He said he'd have a comfort home until after he knew that she had come on that train.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would have happened if she'd come up visiting?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, he never turned his back on her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she would come to see him?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh yes. She was up there every now and then ordering him around. Maybe she'd get him slaughtered. He brought him in. Never had... He was always polite but very, very cold. Now this isn't the same dentist that got mixed up with Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hinkle's wife?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No. No, no, no. That fellow got shot in the leg. Or no, he'd had typhoid fever. That dentist had had typhoid fever. And something did happen to that dentist.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But I remember them telling him about the shots ringing out. And I don't know. Maybe he didn't get shot. Seems to me he got shot in the leg. This is not Landquist now. This is... No, this is another thing entirely that made us just happen to think of it. She mixed up. That's the trouble. You hear several things, you know. You know, you can easily put them together and they seem like they just all come out in jumble. But this is about Landquist. Now that was clear up to my day, you see. So that I do know something about it. I did meet that old woman down in Lewiston. I saw her enough times that I knew who she was. I never met her. But now, old Granny Hayes as they called her, I knew her. And I never did share the bad feeling that the people around town didn't. I felt sorry for the old lady. She was going around, trudging around, taking care of those three kids. And seeing to it that they weren't into any meanness or anything like that. And she gave them meals, you know. And I remember when I graduated from high school, that she sent me a gift. A white blouse of some kind. She had Mrs. Walter get it for me and sent it to me from her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that the Hayes boys did that gave them such a bad reputation around Troy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: They chased women. And did more than chase women. They were rapists of the worst kind. And that's one thing. And the thing that most people hated them for. And they gambled and took the money from people. But then of course, people can be blamed from gambling with them. And of course, I heard of them only by reputation. I didn't know either one of those men. Thank heaven I didn't know them. And one man married a Norwegian girl by the name of Armstrong. And the Swedes and Norwegians, they don't say much about her but they never forgave him. He took her and used to send men up to the house.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: In other words, he sold his wife. And she dared not say anything or he'd kill the kids and things like that. She soon died. Blessing.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But Mrs. Hayes knew that that woman was a good woman originally. And that's why she took every, made every effort to bring those children up alright. Eleanor married that fellow. And Eleanor was only about 16 I think when she got married. She was kind of a pretty girl. She had bad eyes. Her eyes were bad. But they all had bad eyes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Eleanor married what? Which fellow?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: The fellow that sold the house. Oh. She was the youngest of those children. I think she... I don't know who it was. I state that. Some of the details of that story are missing in my life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the story behind the feud between Sly and Hayes?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, they were both drunken sods. And Hayes had threatened the pain if he ever got drunk again. And to disturb the peace that he was going to throw him in jail and let him rot in jail and all that kind of things. So I heard. But I remember I was a little girl five or six years old when that happened.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But that didn't keep these boys. They didn't have their dad to fight for them. I don't know how old I was. I might have been quite a bit older. I wasn't too much older than that. I wouldn't have been that old. It was 1904 when Hayes got shot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it 1904?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: That I would have been eight years old. I was born in 1896. That would have made me eight years old. That's how it goes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who shot him?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Hayes shot... Pain, Sly, shot Hayes. But Hayes was shooting at Pain too. And he hollered at him to come down. Here was the law, ordering him to come down the hill. And Pain said, go back Ann, go back. You don't need to come up here. And the little girl, Pain had married one of these women, these strange women that had come to town to be in one of those houses that they used to have in Troy. And they had flourished, according to the stories that I've heard about it. They had flourished at the time when the railroad was coming in, and the things that he wanted to know about Troy. Well, they had houses like that, and it was a rough town. And drinking, a terrible amount of drinking went on.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And one of the women that was involved in all this, was what Mavis wanted me to tell you about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that on?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Don't let it stop. You don't have to use names if you don't want to. Well, there was a woman there that ran one of them, that was related to a very fine woman. One of the nicest ladies in the country, and one that did the most for the people. For many people. And here she had a sister who... Well, funny enough, that woman had a heart. She did a lot too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had a big heart?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: A big heart. For people who were in trouble. She took care of a lot of people who were in trouble. Yes, she did. Regardless of what people thought of her, and I didn't think much of her naturally, but she would take care of them. She was married, and her husband had to leave her, of course. She ran him off, that was the story. But she was mixed up in that kind of business. She was the madam of one of the houses, in other words. Yes, only she ran the hotel. She turned the hotel into that kind of house, which was doubly bad. And her husband went off, he was a miner in the beginning, and he went off up to Stites.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And got interested in a mine up there, and made money on this mine. Well, after years, she got married to another fellow, named Miller, and she left town. And they said, conducted herself the same way, up in Stites, which had in turn become, you know what fills my nose is the smoke. It's always dead, you know. And I didn't figure that out until after you'd gone the other night. I was talking about what in the world it could be. And it is the cigarette smoke.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'm telling tales out of school now, I don't know the name of any names. But anyway, she married, and I don't know how old she was when she married. And there was one girl who was just my age, a little bit younger than I. And then she married and she had this little girl, awful cute little girl, pretty little girl. She was a great, big, good looking woman. Very good looking woman. Hard to know that she'd been such a little bat. And then that man died. But what I was going to tell in the first question, that when her first husband died, she was notified, he didn't have any other relatives out here. And she got their son to come, she knew where he was. And he came, that boy, he had sent him away someplace to be raised by another relative. And to get out of this brothel.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: So, she sent for him to come and she took the youngest girl. They went to the funeral and I heard some other people coming about it that knew her. And they said that she was Chief Warner and she'd just mourn nobody's business. And she followed him to the cemetery and she told the minister what a good man he was, how good he was, and oh, just all kinds of things. And they said she was genuine about it. They all felt that she really was genuine. And they didn't accuse her of any crocodile tears either. And she mourned this other man too, I guess. Well, she went over to Seattle and I don't know what she did over there. I don't know at all, I'd hear from her once in a while because I knew this sister. And she had a baby born to her there when she was 53 years old, I'll never forget that. That's getting pretty old.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I saw her at a funeral once. And that child was, I heard her telling how old he was. I think she said he was 11 years old. Good healthy little child.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did these girls come from, from the houses?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, heaven only knows, I don't know that. I don't know that. I don't know a thing about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The cities?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I don't know. I don't know. Those two hays were reared right here and gotten ready for it. But, I don't know. And down at Lewiston, Lewiston was a tough place for a long, long time.
