There were people going through there, it was a river port, you see. They used to have the boats going on the river all the time. And there were people staying over. For example, Now this would be part of Nesbir's history.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you're not taking Nesbir's history, are you?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: If it's interesting enough, you can say it. Oh, yes. This would be interesting enough. Well, tell them. No, I'm not telling you. I wouldn't put this on record. I'll tell you a part of that, I'll tell you the part of that man, the Lato County part of it, I'll tell you all of that that I know. And I don't know enough of that. Well, see, if it's related to the county, if it has to do with it, then it's... Even if it happened to Nesbir's, like Madame Loselle from Soiseign, she went down to Lewiston, but she came from... She was around here, so that's... But you go ahead and tell what you want.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, at one time... But please don't put this on the record.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Is it on?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yeah, but that's... Well, I'm not going to tell it, but then I don't know.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I will. J .P. Vollmer was supposed to have been the richest man in Idaho, and when I went to school and got my geography, it was said that he was. They also said that his wife had been a hurdy -gurdy girl. And the hurdy -gurdy girls were written up in Idaho history. By the way, C .J. Brosnan wrote the Idaho history that we used in the schools.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I think there were some errors. I mean, they did not book two, a few that were made, but then I don't believe they were important to them. But anyway, the hurdy -gurdy girls, they were a bunch of entertainers that came in. And of course, they were the kind of girls who supplied these places. Some of them didn't leave the town. I expect some of them took up their abode in these places. Of course, I don't know that. I wasn't around. I wasn't old enough to be around them, believe me. That's one of the reasons I wasn't... My folks didn't want me to get away from home to go to school even. It used to be that they just didn't like to see such things happen, but by the time I was 16, I figured that if you were living with decent people, you would know why you had a chance to be as good as anybody else. And there was one thing about it. Those bad places were segregated. They were kept away from the people, the so -called respectable people. They were very carefully shielded from them. And it was said that, of course, these hurdy -gurdy girls, they had their own little audience. There was entertainment, and then there was entertainment backstage and so on. And the story was that J .P. Vollmer and the dentist... I don't apply to remember him, the name, because I never did know him or anything about him, but his name was Squires, S -Q -U -I -E -R -S. Now, I've known a lot of S -Q -U -I -R -E -S, but they are different. That was the only one that I ever knew that spelled his name like that. And they had a couple of boys, and one of them was a dentist. Squires. But J .P. Vollmer and the Squires went together, and they married a couple of her year -year. So Mrs. J .P. Vollmer had had a history. But of course, that was forgotten and forgiven. Sure. You don't get married to the richest man in the county. And not drop your past. You can drop it if you have enough money.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I remember that her name was Sally, and I heard a man say once, they used to call her Sal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear that?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: J .P. Vollmer's wife was a pretty good girl. I heard you say it just before I went to... Well, the Vollmers had five children. Bess, the oldest girl, was married to A .E. Clark, and that's the house that Nell has.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I know I have that, because Mrs. Sterling Davis had found the son of the man who had been here. It was changed to Moscow, and what he said about it. It's in the manuscript.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It is?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: So Vollmer had... he and the Pacific were having a disagreement. That was part of the reason for the name change. Well, part of the reason. Of course, some people wanted to... He talked about other things afterwards. And he said one of the reasons was that a lot of people were dissatisfied with it, as far as I was concerned. But they weren't only dissatisfied with it because they couldn't say the name, he said. Partly because they were dissatisfied with Vollmer's tactics. And he went around building up these mills. Now you ask how he got to be so wealthy. Well now, I knew, as I said, I stayed with Mrs. Eastwood, who was his niece. Wait a minute. Yes, his sister's daughter. And I knew Mrs. Crutinger, his sister, very well. And she was an older woman. She was Mrs. Eastwood's mother, and lived right next door to her. I used to talk to her lots about early days. She told me so many things. But, uh... She used to say to me... I know this poem, I'm just going to have trouble again. She said that, now they had had some money. And I do not remember that his sister told me the source of their money. But their parents had died. And, uh... I believe they were a German descent, a German extraction. Quite sure they were.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And, uh... The children were left to his guardianship. And he brought them west. And I don't even remember where they'd come from. That might be in that manuscript, I'm not sure. I'm not sure either. But you're going to say how he actually wound up making the money. Well, he might have used, and did use, the inheritance money, no doubt. But he had to take care of these sisters. Now there was at least Mrs. Crutinger, and her sister, Mrs. Trescot, who also lived in Lewiston. Mrs. Crutinger had one daughter. That was her daughter, Lula. And she was Mrs. Eastwood. Mrs. E. Eastwood in Lewiston. And she's the one I live with. Mrs. Eastwood. Not the world of. Excuse me. And, uh, Mrs. Crutinger was her mother, and she was married to a man named Alfred Crutinger. And then Mrs. Trescot. Trescot was her second husband, because her first husband had been Shearer. And she had two daughters by... Shearer, and Virginia Shearer was a teacher in the Lewiston schools for many, many years. In the Lewiston High School. And then she had a daughter named Bess. That seemed to have been a family name. They all had Bess's. And I remember Mrs. Crutinger told why, but I... But I don't know what that was. So there was Bess Shearer. She married a man named Bodine. Well now this, uh, Shearer and Bodine, and Crutinger, they all had money too, and they'd go in partners with the richest men in the state of Idaho. And they got sheep, and they just peopled the hills around Lewiston with sheep. And made money and oafest. Mutton and wool. And they sent their wool away. And that's when sheep sharing became a great industry around Lewiston.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And of course, where did he fit into it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He'd loaned them money. And charged them interest. He got the interest, and he also had an interest in the sheep farming itself, so he made money. Money, money, money. And all his partners had to... They took the brunt of the sheep sharing. J .P. Vollmer didn't go out and herd any sheep.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And then came the panic of 93. There was a U .S. panic of 93, and this country suffered. And then, to add to the burdens of the people here, and especially the farmers, there was a farm crop failure. And they were unable to get their crops harvested. And they tell, and I've heard Papa tell and Mr. Roberts tell, how the grain just sprouted in the shock. And they did try to raise pigs.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But, and Vollmer got in on that pig raising too, they said. Helped some farmers to get them so they could get out and eat this grain, but not all of it. But, he'd given mortgages on these farms. They got so they couldn't live. They had to, they'd had a failure in the spring, and it had been a wet spring, and a lot of them had gotten in the seed, and they'd had to buy seed, so they'd gone to him for money. He established these little banks all over, and also farm mills, because there was a railroad coming in, and they'd have a way of shipping out their grain, and a way of shipping out their flour. So if they got flour mills, and got millers in here, to mill that wheat, they'd get more money than they would out of shipping the wheat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the use of letting them, or sending them back the money for milling the wheat back to some farmer in the east, or some miller in the east?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No purchase in that. So we had Vollmer flour, and I remember they used to have Vollmer's. Vollmer's fair water mills on the sacks of flour. Used it all over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then these farmers, how did they get their money?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, they mortgaged their farm to get more money to put in more seed, or to buy stock. And they bought stock, and then they couldn't feed them. And they had to sell them for a little or nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what happened?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Vollmer foreclosed.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And he had good partners. Kootenay'sher was one of them. Miss Kootenay'sher didn't say much about that, but she did say Alfred. Bread, she always called him Bread. Bread was one. And then a fellow named Fix. And Fix bought the ones, mainly, that went on Fix Ridge, and that's where Fix Ridge got its name. That's from the fix that that fix. That's that fix. Not some other fix, but that fix. And then the family finally went to Spokane. And they had one daughter and about six sons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he was a partner of Vollmer's?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh yes! All those guys were partners of his. And of course Vollmer got the lion's share of everything.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And uh... So that's why the people around Troy still don't remember him too fondly, Vollmer. That's right. That's right. He didn't show too much... He didn't take as many for a ride around Troy, and get up there and take as many for a ride as he had out other places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I don't know. Well, it was better settled, and they were better able to take care of themselves. They were better able to fend for themselves, and it was a race of hardy pioneers. A lot of them had the same spirit Mrs. Roberts did. They weren't going to be taken in by any...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did the people trust him not to foreclose when they took out the mortgages with him?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I suppose so. I guess they didn't know that he was going to do that. They never had a panic and a cop figure at the same time. And a money panic in the United States where they couldn't get a cent of money from anybody else. That was back before the days of the... before Woodrow Wilson and his bank. And Franklin D. and the moratorium on banks and so on. That was the day before that. And the people back there had those... they allowed panic stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were young, did you hear talk about Vollmer that put him in a bad light around Troy? Or is this just more recent than that?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I didn't hear very much about him. I didn't circulate much. I was out on the farm, and I didn't get out much. Papa said he was terrible. He went out and solicited these. That's the way Papa felt that he solicited people to come in and take money. Yes, at the bank. He'd see to it that he didn't let them have money. And then he didn't say he was going to foreclose on the mortgage. When they got up there to get the money, he couldn't let them have money without security. Well, they didn't have any security. They'd had to sell their livestock because they didn't have anything to feed them. They'd had a crop fair. I heard that. Believe me. I've heard that he charged too high interest. Oh, he did. I don't know what the rates were. I was a child. I didn't even get around to that. I listened wide -eyed to a lot of it. But I didn't get around to get down to real economics. I didn't know what economics were. So you're getting this word from somebody that's pretty young at that time. I'm not young now, but I was then. And I'd have a different story now, believe me, if I could get ahold of that old J .P. Walmer. I'll care for him a bit. But I thought, my goodness, he's quite a man to amass such a fortune. They had a big house in Lewiston. And they had a big wall around it. Well, I can see why they walled themselves in. In the first place, I guess maybe she needed to wall herself in. And she had a nice cultivated garden and she got a gardener to come and take care of it. It was divided in about half. But half of it, and I did see that from over the wall. And they had a wall around it. Big fence around it. High fence. You saw the top of the house, of course. And then this untended garden. But Mrs. Eastwood told me about it and some of his students did. That he had the idea that you should just plant stuff and let it go untended like it would in the wild. That's what he wanted to see. He wanted to see the wildwood. And he got some benches and stuck them around inside that. So they said, because he just wanted to go out in the woods. And he'd have a wildwood there on either one of the places. It was a big lot. A great big place. I don't know how it was. Right at the top of the 5th Street grade. Or close to the top. That's where the house was. I guess the house was torn away. The wall was torn away a long time ago. Then they had five children. They had this Bess who married A .E. Clark and he was put in as the president of the Vollmer Clearwater Valley Bank. And he was a banker who knew how to make money. And he succeeded, little dried up shrimp. But he married Bess Vollmer, the oldest girl. Well, they made money, but they never had any children. And, didn't do them any good. The house now is going to somebody else. My friend, Anna Follett, owns it. Made an apartment house out of it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And, I can't help saying to some of my good Republican friends, they lived to see a lot of things wither on the vine. I wish I could trace them. I wish Nixon was withering a little more quickly.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And then they had Genevieve. She married a guy named Bonner. Well, I don't know how they ever came as Bonner kids, but none of them ever made a name for themselves. For either good or bad, as far as I know. And the third one was Norma. Norma Gina, I guess. Norma. No, Norma. She was the third girl, and she was just about Mrs. Eastwood's age. And, she was a pretty good looking woman, I remember seeing her. But she married a doctor, Dr. Scott Bruce Hopkins. Bruce Hopkins was a hyphenated last name. Bruce Hopkins. And one Bruce Hopkins became a doctor, like his father. He was a doctor in Spokane. That's who she married. Well, she died years ago. She died early. And there were two boys. I don't know why they came in the line up. I don't remember that. I remember Mrs. Eastwood telling her, except I do remember that Bess was the oldest. I remember the order of the girls, but I don't know whether the boys were any dispersed or not. But the very youngest one was Norman Balmer. And he was crazy, supposedly. That's what they called it. But I suppose maybe mentally ill. I don't know what it was. I don't know. But he married a school teacher from Coeur d 'Alene. A girl who went to the normal. And she had to divorce him. Oh, they had a terrible time. And I heard there were all kinds of stories connected with that. He'd been taken to a sanitarium, and that was the last I ever heard of him. I never did trace him through. I don't know what became of him. That would have to be looked up. It may be later on that somebody did look him up. Because looking up all these Idaho encyclopedias, you can find the Balmer family. And I've done it a time or two in the past, but I suppose it would tell. Ralston had a family, and he lived over in Clarkson for a long time, but not nothing did anything worthwhile. Ralston didn't himself. They were the Balmers. So they didn't amount to anything. That's just the thing built up okay. I want to ask you a couple more things about Hayes and that business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One thing, did you say before that you think that Payne hated the Hayes boys because they had been sort of after his wife?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I didn't say that. I don't believe. But they might have been, but because they were terrible. They were women chasers and things like that. I don't know though. I don't know that. Yeah, I sort of thought you had, but maybe you didn't. No, I'm sure I didn't because I don't know it. If I did, I misspoke myself because I don't know that. I could well believe it. But I know that she was a woman who had come in and was one of these people that wasn't regarded very highly. And she had a little girl. That was hers. It wasn't Payne's. And this little girl had run out in the yard and said, She was Papa's killing Mama or something to that effect. And screaming and crying. Screaming loudly. And somebody told Hayes. So Hayes came out and yelled at Payne, Payne get down here. If you don't, I'm coming up to get you. Well Payne just asked him to come on up. So I don't know. I understood that he started. That he started to go up the hill. But anyway, Payne just shot at him and he was standing at the corner there at the hotel. And for a long, long while a sideboard was, they'd point to him and say, there's the shots that Payne put through that sideboard. I understood that, I've heard tell that Hayes' death was not mourned by the people. It wasn't. I can, I'll testify to that. That's what I heard from everybody. I lived out in the country and I heard the country people saying that. And then as the years have gone on and people have discussed it, and the Hayes come up with it, a lot of people will say that. Or they'll say, well that's another thing now. That's another legacy that the Hayes left us. You know, trouble if they'd stirred up. So I'm sure that that is a correct thing. However, I never knew one of those men. Why I should be living to relate their awful story. You did tell me though about how Al Roberts and your father got involved in the events of that day that night. That night they, you see, we didn't have telephones I guess. You have the date for that. Nineteen four. Nineteen four. No, we didn't have telephones. A telephone, we got a telephone through, a long distance telephone through Spokane and went through to Lewiston and it went over American Ridge. They didn't want to go down through the canyon. And it went over land and that was in 1909. I remember I got that date. So we didn't have any telephones. But somebody always came, Mr. Roberts of course was always a messenger. He wasn't in town but they'd always come and tell him because he'd tell him what to do. He was kind of, you know, the man that said what we'll do. He was the Republican head man and Mr. Harlan who lived on the other side of us was the Democrat and Mr. Mushlitz was the Democrat. Well I recognized, even as a little girl, that Mushlitz was the brain of all of them and I knew that as far as actual brain was concerned, by the time I was twelve years old I knew who was brain, who wasn't. And I knew Mr. Harlan knew more than Mr. Roberts. Mr. Roberts was an awful nice little, I liked Mr. Roberts better. But he was a bluff, hearty good fellow. He'd been brought up Republican so he was still Republican. But Mr. Harlan studied the Populist Party and he knew about all these different things. I heard about that from him. Well, I became a Democrat when I was preparing for debates in high school and I think I weighed the brains of the people that I'd met and I knew who were the brain ones and who weren't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In spite of Wade King saying to me, you know, church me less being a Democrat because he says, what do you think Al Roberts would do, or your dad?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I said, you know, good Republicans. Oh shucks. I said, well they'd say, well I guess you're right. I said, they've said it many times to me Wade.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'd like to talk about that later, about the politics, but what about the night that Al Roberts... Well, it was the first time of the night it was, but I know that we were all asleep and I was sound asleep. I heard this terrible knocking at the door. And Mr. Roberts' voice, and believe me, when I heard him saying Charlie, Charlie, Charlie, in an excited voice I knew something was on. So I woke up and was all ears. I remember that very well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Papa said, what's the matter now Al?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And he said, Charlie get up. Get your clothes on. He said, we've got to go down to Old Man Sly's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Go down to Old Man Sly's?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I don't ever have to go down to Old Man Sly's. Well, a dad said, I'm getting mixed up thinking about my husband. Papa said, you don't have to, we don't have to do that. He said, I... It'd be delirium treatments because the old man was known to be an awful drinker. He'd come out yelling. I suppose he's come home yelling. Well, he says they brought him home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who brought him home?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, he named some fellas around there. I don't remember. And they had to bring him home because Paine killed Old Man Hayes. He did. That woke Papa real good. Killed Old Man Hayes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And then he told the story about the little girl yelling that Papa's killing Mama. And Old Man Hayes challenging him and Paine started down with his gun and he just dropped him. Dropped Hayes, I mean. And he said Old Man Hayes was going up there and said he would kill him. Well, six of them, doesn't know the other fellas. Papa said, Paine's no good, but the Old Man Hayes is no good either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And did he get either one of those good for nothing boys?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No. The cowards, they stayed out of the way. They didn't go after their father's aid at all. So, that was that. But Papa got up and went, reluctantly, but he went. He was quite interested after he heard this. And they went down there and by that time they had the old man quieted. I think they'd gotten a doctor there to quiet him. Gave him more liquor or something. It's funny to me that they had to go all the way out on the ridge to get people to quiet Sly down. Oh, well Sly came out there. That's where he lived. He was a neighbor. He was a neighbor, you see. Oh no, Papa wouldn't have gone to town to quiet anybody. But they lived less than a mile from us by the cut -off. And of course Papa and Mr. Roberts knew the cut -off road, so they went shortcut to Sly's place. They'd gone around the road that had been a couple of miles. But they didn't do that. And of course, Papa thought it was all foolishness going down Sly's to quiet him down. And the old lady, she was going to go in and they said that the old lady Hayes was wild and she said she'd kill pain and she kills pain and I'll kill her. That's what I'll do old lady Sly said. She was quiet woman too. You said that she was sort of laying for Sly after he got out of prison.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes. People said that's what she was doing. We started going down past our house and people said she was going out on those roads to get pain. And when pain got out of the pen and came up, he had a car. And she said he couldn't ride around. She'd stop him doing that. And she came with this little old cart that her three young children drove around a lot. And she drove every now and then out in the country. She was out and got cherries from Papa one year, driving in that kind of a two -wheeled cart with this one white horse. I remember it was a white horse. One horse cart. But she did and she said she'd kill him when she had her gun or not. But people were kind of waiting to hear she did. She'd kill Sly but she didn't. And there were a lot of people that said that he protects Sly. They thought they'd been a lot more peaceful since old man Hayes had gotten killed. No, I don't think anybody... there were very many people that learned Hayes passing. How long did...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: was pain in prison then?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I don't know. It wasn't very long. I don't know how long it was. Five or six years. Six or seven years. Maybe. I don't know if it was that long or not. You know, I don't know. I just can't remember that. I know it wasn't very long. I'd hear people say, well, they shouldn't have let him out. And then there were others that said, well, I didn't know why.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was his wife still there when he got out?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No. She went away. She went to Boise to be near him for a long while or for a while. But I don't think she went back to him. I think he married another woman. I think she... I think that's the story. I'm not quite sure about that. I guess I got so I wasn't too interested in things lie after that first debacle. Because as I remember it, you know, I don't seem to know about things like that. But I'm quite sure he had another wife.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And they said that he was just outraged and so mad at that kid. That little girl. Yeah, the little girl. I told on him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the mother frightened for the child's life then?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I would think if he... Oh, I don't know. I don't know that she knew enough to be frightened for her. She and Payne were in a fight. They were in a drunken fight. She was a drunken stoop too. So they said, of course I didn't know her. And that's wrong for me to say that because I don't... didn't know her. I couldn't say that for sure. But that's what the people of that time said.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I'm...
